text:
Program:The World Today

Presented by

The curtain has closed on Australia's record breaking performance at the Paris Games.At a star-studded closing ceremony inside France's national stadium, Australian athletes celebrated their stellar achievements while musicians played and fireworks exploded overhead.As organisers look ahead to Brisbane 2032, what funding will be needed to ensure our continued success and could that become an issue in a cost of living crisis?

Featured:Tracy Holmes, Professorial Fellow in Sport, University of Canberra, and host, Sports Ambassador Podcast.Adjunct Professor Colin Dwyer, James Cook UniversityClayton Cook, Managing Director, Human Capital

A display of the Olympic rings in front of the Eiffel Tower.(AP: Francois Mori)

Rachel Mealey:The curtain has closed on Australia's record-breaking performance at the Paris Games. At a star-studded closing ceremony inside France's National Stadium, Australian athletes celebrated their stellar achievements while musicians played and fireworks exploded overhead. As organisers look ahead to Brisbane 2032, what funding will be needed to ensure our continued success and could that become an issue in a cost-of-living crisis? Nick Grimm reports.

Nick Grimm:Australia's Olympians have kicked back and relaxed. The flame has been extinguished and the Olympic flag lowered, folded and passed on to 2028 host city Los Angeles. And after Australia's most successful games ever, the conversation now shifts to how to replicate that winning formula.

Tracy Holmes:You know, what it says, when you look at the medal tally and you look at the nations that finish on top and Australia has finished in fourth, it shows that the countries that invest most money in sport win the most medals. Unfortunately, it's that simple.

Nick Grimm:Tracey Holmes is a professorial fellow in sport at the University of Canberra and host of the Sports Ambassador podcast. As she points out, right now the nation is cashing in on efforts to revamp the Australian Sports Commission and an athlete-focused investment that it's hoped will keep on delivering for another two games at least.

Tracy Holmes:All of this aimed on what people are calling the green and golden runway to Brisbane 2032 and so what we've seen is that with more of an intense focus and a real honing in on exactly what athletes need in order to get the best performance out of themselves in these big moments, it does pay dividends. Obviously, it costs a lot of money.

Nick Grimm:But in a cost of living crisis and with billions of dollars of infrastructure still needing to be built, there's going to be plenty of competition for the funding needed to bring home gold when Australia again hosts the Olympics eight years from now. Adjunct Professor Colin Dwyer is with Queensland's James Cook University.

Colin Dwyer:You know, we've only got eight years in Brisbane in order to get all the stadiums built, to get all the railways built, to get all the roads built and to get the athletes village built, to get the media centre built. There are hotels and accommodation facilities that need to be built to accommodate not just the athletes but also the spectators and the officials as well. So you can see where all this is going. We're now looking at, on my table, around about $62 billion worth of direct and indirect and induced expenditure that's required to build or put the Brisbane Olympics on. Now, that's different to what Paris is because Paris had 95% of its stadiums and infrastructure in place and I believe that Los Angeles is in a similar position. But we have to build the stadiums in Brisbane and we have to build the indirect transport facilities to accommodate all the people that will be coming.

Nick Grimm:And that's likely to require an Olympian effort just to get things ready to host the games.

Clayton Cook:So where is the talent, where's the talent, you know, where are the people, the skills going to come from to get this infrastructure, these projects done?

Nick Grimm:Clayton Cook is the Managing Director of Townsville-based TP Human Capital. He argues workforce shortages mean it's not just athletes that will need to be fast-tracked between now and the Brisbane Games.

Clayton Cook:I think that's a great analogy. You hit the nail on the head. I mean, not only do we need people, but we need the best people, just like an Olympics team. So, you know, to get these projects done and done well and so that we're leading the world stage. But I have concerns with the fact that our economy has been performing well and we've got skill shortages everywhere with projects nationally outside of the Olympics that need to be done. So how are we going, you know, there needs to be more thought into the skills, talent, training, development of our skills base so that we can get these projects done.

Nick Grimm:The Federal Government says it's committed a record amount of spending for athletes, including $250 million for the Australian Institute of Sport in Canberra. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese.

Anthony Albanese :I think it will be a fantastic time in the lead up to 2032. And we, of course, have just had our best ever Olympic Games, but I'm confident we can do even better into the future.

Rachel Mealey:Prime Minister Anthony Albanese ending that report by Nick Grimm.

title: Can Australia replicate its Olympics success?	 - ABC listen
description: The curtain has closed on Australia's record breaking performance at the Paris Games.
keywords: podcast, Paris Olympics, Los Angeles 2028, Brisbane 2023, sports commission, athlete, infrastructure, workforce
